_README: THE SEARCH SPACE, AS DATA. Every bound, default, probability and sim
literal the search uses lives here -- nothing is hardcoded in the code.
Agents (and you) edit this file to change what is discoverable.
Anything absent from a config file falls back to these values.
Every run records the exact space it used in its results row, so a
survivor can always be traced back to the bar it had to clear.
RULE OF THUMB: if the search cannot sample it, it cannot find it. A
weight cap of 3.0 means a setup needing w=5 is invisible AND the search
will never tell you it was looking through a keyhole. Widen deliberately.

grid._doc: How the data grid is built. Changing any of these builds (and caches) a different grid.
grid._interval_doc: The LOW timeframe the trades are made on. The cascade's 'dial down' target. Try 5m/15m/1h.
grid._gate_doc: WHICH criteria the daily trend means (e.g. sma50_over_200@1d, above_50ma@4h, or 'none'). This selects the definition; gate_mode decides whether it is a law or an opinion.
grid._gate_mode_doc: 'factor' = the trend is carried ONLY by the weighted `trend` factor, so w_trend decides how much it matters (0 = ignore, large = de facto gate). 'hard' = the old behaviour, the gate ANDs the signal and no weight can escape it. NOTE: 'catching the knife' (timing the reversal of a major downtrend) is ONLY testable in 'factor' mode -- a hard trend gate vetoes every knife entry by definition, since you are buying while the trend is down.
grid._fib_anchor_doc: WHICH timeframe the fib trend anchor (p1/p2/p3 -> retracement levels, the fib stop ladder, fib_prox) is drawn from. 'self' = the trading timeframe (re-anchors every ~35 bars, so its levels are ones Paul would never have drawn); '4h'/'1d'/'1w' = anchor there and forward-fill (shifted to the higher bar's CLOSE, so no lookahead). Default 1d because Paul anchors high and those levels persist -- his BTC 53422->109028 anchor was still working 7 months later. Lower timeframes stay available on purpose: day-trading setups must be testable too. Which anchor wins is a QUESTION FOR THE SEARCH, not an assumption -- run a job per anchor and compare (grid_sig keeps them apart).

_doc: FACTORS AS DATA. Each entry compiles to a signed ~[-1,1] plane and joins the factor stack, so it gets a weight and is instantly searchable. A SETUP IS A WEIGHT VECTOR, NOT CODE: 'catching the knife' = nonzero weights on sell_exhaustion + holds_200ema + buy_surge.

SIGN IS EXPLICIT and it matters: weights are sampled non-negative, so the definition must say what the factor MEANS. Falling sell-volume is bullish; falling price is not -- the op cannot know which you meant. Use "sign": -1 to flip.
ops: falling, rising, cross_up, cross_down, dist_above, prox, stall, hh_hl, fails_to_break, column. See weisswave/factors.py.
src/ref are signal columns (Close, volumeup, volumedn, ema200, sma50, wt1, rci_l, fib_p2, ...) or a plain number. Never an expression -- a strategy file is untrusted input and must never be eval'd.

NOTE: these are read from THIS file at import (portfolio_multi builds FACTOR_NAMES from it). --space=FILE swaps the other knobs but not the factor list; edit here to change what exists. The grid cache keys on this spec, so a change rebuilds rather than silently reusing the old layout.

pivot_confirm turns Pine's lbR into a WEIGHT. lbR is applied to a bar that already happened -- pivothigh/pivotlow only DECLARE a pivot once enough right-side bars prove it was an extreme -- so lbR is both how much evidence you demand AND how late you act, and those fight. Frozen at one value it is a permanent guess. Emitted as evidence, the search prices it: a 1-bar-old pivot contributes little (clears the threshold only when other factors agree), a 6-bar-old one contributes fully but arrives late. Do NOT use sign:-1 to mean 'prefer early' -- 0 means no candidate, not maximum earliness. Earliness lives in the weight and the entry threshold.

sell_exhaustion._doc: Sell pressure fading over the last 5 bars. Bullish, hence sign +1.

holds_200ema._doc: Price's signed distance above the 200 EMA. +1 = 2% above, -1 = 2% below. 'the price doesnt hammer below the 200ema, it holds'.

buy_surge._doc: Buying volume took over from selling volume within 3 bars.

coil._doc: Higher highs AND higher lows -- compression before expansion.

absorption._doc: Sellers pierced the 200 EMA but the close is back above it: the push failed.

stalling._doc: Price going nowhere. sign -1 because teetering instead of pushing argues AGAINST a long -- the reversal read at a fib level.

build._doc: PAUL'S 'BUILDS BEFORE MOVES', as a number. 1 - |net move| / total path travelled: 0 = a straight line, 1 = price travelled hard and ended where it started. Distinct from `stalling`, which has NO PATH TERM and therefore scores 'sat still' and 'churned and came back' identically (both 1.0) -- opposite situations, and Paul trades the second. IT IS THE RATIO, NOT THE VOLUME: relative volume on his builds is 44th-57th percentile, i.e. ordinary. The effort/result read is right but the DENOMINATOR collapses; there is no extra effort. Raw wasted travel does not work either -- it scales with the window and ranks his excluded example highest. Only the ratio separates them. *** `bars` IS THE SEARCH AXIS, NOT A SETTING. *** 20 is a STARTING POINT ONLY. Paul's five labelled builds run 8, 15, 44, 64 and 89 bars -- an 11x spread -- and at a fixed 20 they read 85th/64th/93rd percentile, which is noise. At native length they read 98th/94th/87th/86th, and the one he called 'a different type' correctly falls to 54th. Sweep it (--set=factors.build.bars=N; one grid per value). Leaving it at 20 rebuilds RNG_LOOK and throws the finding away. sign is +1 and left FOR THE SEARCH: whether churn is bullish (accumulation before the break) or bearish is the question, not the assumption -- `stalling` ships sign:-1 and has already answered it the other way, from a literal.

wt_depth._doc: HOW STRETCHED the oscillator is, as a number instead of a bit. wt_oversold is `wt2 <= -60`: measured over 226,305 bars of 1d that is 15,615 bars whose MEDIAN wt2 is -68 and whose DEEPEST is -322 -- a 5x range handed to the search as ONE BIT, plus 4.9% of all bars sitting within +-5 of the line and flipping on a coin. This keeps the magnitude. sign=-1 flips the axis so DEEP OVERSOLD reads POSITIVE (bullish): wt2=-60 -> +0.76, wt2=+60 -> -0.76. That is legitimate here because 0 means 'at the midline', a real neutral -- unlike pivot_confirm where 0 means 'no candidate' and sign=-1 would make empty bars score highest. `scale` is Paul's own os/ob level (60), not a new constant. It is DATA: sweep it. tanh not clip, so -60 and -322 stay different numbers.

wt_gap._doc: THE FORCE BEHIND THE CROSS. wt1-wt2 is the histogram; it spans +-630 (sd 53) and ONLY ITS SIGN CHANGE is consumed today, as the boolean wt_cross_up. The magnitude -- how hard the cross fired -- is discarded entirely. A cross that barely happened and one that snapped over are the same bool. sign=+1: a positive gap (wt1 above wt2) is the bullish side, which is what wt_cross_up already asserts by existing. Whether it matters is the weight's job. `scale` is DATA.

wt_slope._doc: IS THE OSCILLATOR TURNING? Not used anywhere today -- d(wt1) is computed by nothing. A cross at -300 still falling and a cross at -65 turning up are opposite setups wearing the same boolean. COMPOSES WITH wt_depth: depth x slope as two weights is 'deep AND turning', which is Paul's never-catch-a-falling-knife rule expressed as a dial rather than a law -- and unlike a hard trend gate it does NOT veto the knife by construction, so it is testable. `bars` and `scale` are DATA; bars=1 is a starting point, not a setting.

_doc_indicators: INDICATOR COMBINATIONS ARE NOW SEARCHED, not chosen. Each indicator below is its own weighted factor, so WHICH COMBINATION fires is decided by WHICH WEIGHTS ARE NON-ZERO -- w=0 means that indicator is out of the strategy. The search discovers 'tdi_long + adp_bull_div' instead of being handed it in bot_strategies.json, and conf_threshold decides how many must agree.
This is 'weights not gates' taken all the way down. It is also cheap: every factor precomputes into ONE grid and the weights sweep at sim time.

Some of these come from the proprietary signal suite (combined.py) and will not exist in a public checkout. A missing column is zeroed with a named warning at grid build, not a crash -- but a zeroed factor is a DEAD search dimension, so trim the list to what your signal layer actually has.
_doc_filters: FILTERS ARE FACTORS TOO. 'Trade with the trend' stops being a law the search cannot see and becomes a weight it has to EARN. w_flt_sma50_over_200 = 0 tests the strategy with that filter OFF; a large weight makes it a de facto gate. Which screen works best -- sma50_over_200 vs above_50ma vs the RCI ichimoku read -- is now an empirical question the search answers, which is what Paul has been asking for since the start.
Note these are 0/1 columns, so they only ever ARGUE FOR a long (never against). Use sign:-1 on a copy if you want the absence to push back.

flt_sma50_over_200._doc: sma50 > sma200. ONE LINE, and it never looks at price -- it is purely whether the two SMAs are stacked, i.e. the STATE of which golden_cross is the EVENT. Called `minervini` until 2026-07-14, which promised the 8-criterion Trend Template and delivered this; the name made it read as authoritative and it became the default gate everywhere unexamined. The real template (price > 150MA > 200MA, rising 200MA, within 25% of the 52w high, RS rank) is a DIFFERENT screen and is not built.

spr_confirm._doc: The SPR question as a dial instead of a constant. spr is a bear divergence on volumedn confirmed at a frozen lbR=1 -- the weakest possible evidence, one bar. This emits how much right-side evidence the volumedn peak has actually survived (0=none or beaten, 1.0=cap bars). lbL=6/cap=6 mirror the spr pivot family in combined.py. Tune lbL/cap here; the WEIGHT decides how much confirmation the setup demands.

_doc: INDICATOR SENSITIVITY -- what makes a signal fire in the first place. These were defaults buried THREE CALLS DEEP (load_universe -> build_signals -> combined_signals -> tdi_signals) and the signals cache did not key on them, so 'make what fires the green candle more sensitive' was not merely hard, it was inexpressible -- and the cache would have hidden any attempt by serving frames built with the old numbers.

GOLDEN = volume_cross_up AND tdi_long. So the green candle's sensitivity is `tdi.oversold` (how deep the RSI band must be) plus `pullback` (how eagerly the Weis wave flips, which drives volumeup/volumedn and therefore volume_cross_up). MEASURED at the shipped defaults on BTC daily: tdi_long fires 29/2373 bars (1.22%), golden 14/2373 (0.59%) -- roughly twice a year. Whether that is correctly strict or badly mis-set for this timeframe is a QUESTION FOR THE SEARCH.

COST: changing any of these invalidates the signals cache (a ~2min rebuild per interval) and the grid. They are a GRID dimension like gate/fib_anchor -- run a job per setting and compare; grid_sig keeps them apart so results at oversold=40 are never compared to oversold=45.
tdi.* only exists when the proprietary suite is present; it is ignored otherwise.
signals._pullback_doc: Weis wave trend-detection length. Lower = the wave flips more eagerly = more volume_cross_up = more golden candles.

tdi._oversold_doc: tdi_long = crossunder(fast, slow) AND slow < oversold. RAISE it (45, 50) to fire more often / earlier; LOWER it (35) to demand a deeper washout. This is the knob behind 'if I make what fires the green candle more sensitive, does it help or hurt'.

wt._doc: The OVERLAY's WaveTrend (9/13/3 on hlc3) -- NOT the WTV panel's (9/12/3). Two different WaveTrends in one stack; the port had been feeding the panel's series to redDiamond/blood_diamond/wtbuy/wtsell.

pivots._doc: PIVOT SETTINGS PER SIGNAL FAMILY. lbR is the CONFIRMATION LAG -- the 'offset by one bar' in the Pine. lbR too small = fires on a pivot that is not one yet (dangerous). lbR too large = confirmed after the move (worthless). That trade-off was frozen at 1 everywhere; now it is testable. lbL = how significant a swing must be to count as a pivot at all.

signals._pullback1_doc: SECOND wave engine (Combined's 'TDL for buy/sell pressure'). Drives volumeup1/volumedn1 -> crossup -> golden. VERIFIED: at pullback1 == pullback the two engines are byte-identical, so this changes nothing until you move it. It exists so 'what if buy/sell pressure flipped more eagerly than the wave?' is askable at all.

sim._doc: Values passed straight to portsim that were previously literals in agent_search.
sim._htf_screen_doc: The weekly SETUP screen (which names are eligible). 1 = on. The htf_thr threshold is sampled per-config below.
sim._hold_bars_doc: Time-clock exit in bars. 0 = off. A prior session hardcoded 78 (3 trading days) which silently closed 81% of trades, then hardcoded it to 0. It is a knob, not a constant -- sweep it if you want it.
sim._reentry_cooldown_doc: Bars after a STOP-OUT before the same symbol may be re-entered. 0 = off. A signal is a STATE, not an event -- combine_signals keeps it live for `window` bars -- so one signal can fund several entries: stop out, the signal is still inside its window, re-enter, stop out again. MEASURED on BTC 15m/24mo (tdi_long, 3% stop): 135 stops in 252 trades, 20 streaks of 3+ consecutive stops, max streak 8; with a 10% stop the worst 10-trade run was -60%. Dangerous in proportion to timeframe: a bad month on the daily is an afternoon at 15m. Only a STOP arms it; a target/trail exit is a winner banked and re-entry after one is a continuation. SWEEP IT, do not pick it.

space._doc: What rng_cfg samples. Each entry is a distribution the search draws from.

weights._doc: Per-factor weight. mute_prob = chance a factor is zeroed outright.

thr._doc: ENTRY threshold, as a MULTIPLE of the score the sampled weights TYPICALLY produce: sum(w_k * mean|factor_k|), read from the grid's FSCALE. 1.0 = around the average bar, 3.0 = only strong confluence. This must scale by each factor's real magnitude: sparse indicators like adp_bull_div fire on 0.16% of bars while `trend` fires on 100%, so sum-of-weights overestimated the achievable score ~4x and configs stopped trading entirely.

htf._doc: SCREEN threshold, same relative logic. The old absolute uniform(-1.0, 2.5) drew thresholds the weights often could not reach, which is why 27% of the stored configs never traded.

ta._doc: trail activation; repeated values weight the draw

mutate._doc: How the evolutionary step perturbs an elite config.
mutate._sigma_doc: thr/htf sigmas are FRACTIONS (proportional), not absolute steps -- thr scales with the number of factors, so an absolute step breaks whenever the stack changes.

fitness._doc: How a config is ranked in TRAINING. Not the honest number -- that is the holdout.
fitness._worst_fold_penalty_doc: fit = mean_excess + penalty*min(0, worst_fold). Punishes blow-up folds so a mostly-winning config beats a lucky-once one.
fitness._min_trades_doc: Total trades across all folds below this = UNFIT (dropped, never ranked). A config that never trades is not a strategy: its excess computes as 0.0, which would otherwise outrank every config that traded and lost.

benchmark._doc: GOAL #2's third leg. Paul's signals must be compared to time-tested mainstream strategies, not only to buy-and-hold. Each one is run through the SAME engine/stops/costs/slots as any config, so the comparison is apples-to-apples rather than a quoted statistic. Computed ONCE per fold (not per config) so the bar never moves under the search's feet.
benchmark._UNAPPROVED_PLACEHOLDER: THE EXITS BELOW ARE NOT PAUL'S. A hard 8% stop with no trail and no target was invented by an assistant, not chosen by him, and it CONTRADICTS his own rule (max hold 0, let winners run, test trailing-stop variations / sell-X%-at-target). Whatever CAGR the benchmark prints is therefore partly an artifact of an arbitrary exit. Do not present mainstream comparisons as findings until Paul specifies the exits the textbook strategies should get.
benchmark._note: The textbook strategies run UNGATED, as taught. Dressing them up with our own trend filter would flatter us by making the benchmark ours.

curation._doc: The bar a config must clear to be called a survivor (orchestrate.py).
curation._require_beat_mainstream_doc: A survivor must beat buy-and-hold AND the best textbook strategy on the holdout. Set false to fall back to the buy-and-hold-only bar.
curation._min_pos_frac_doc: Fraction of training folds that must be positive.
curation._min_holdout_exc_doc: Must beat buy-and-hold by more than this on the unseen slice.

validation._doc: The honesty chain. agent_search uses wf_folds + holdout; validate.py uses outer_folds + mode + train_frac and is the honest one.
validation._holdout_doc: agent_search's single fixed final slice. KNOWN WEAK: every config ever scored against it spends a little of its evidential value, and survivors() PICKS by it -- which makes it a second training set, not a holdout. It is also one regime (a bear), where the trend filters correctly stand bull strategies down: 75 of 252 configs made ZERO trades there and the median made 7. Use validate.py for the honest number.
validation._mode_doc: validate.py's training window. 'sliding' = fixed-width window that moves, regime-adaptive, discards stale history (Paul's lean: crypto regimes turn over fast). 'anchored' = train always starts at bar 0 and grows, uses all history but assumes 2020 still teaches you about 2026. Kept available on purpose -- which is right is testable.
validation._train_frac_doc: Width of the training window as a fraction of total history (sliding), or where the first split lands (anchored). The remaining history is divided into outer_folds test windows.
